topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt cavernous, the silence amplified by the sheer absence of Mr. Harrison’s familiar, low murmur. I stood by his mahogany desk, the leather blotter cool beneath my fingertips, tracing the outline of his empty chair. A single, neatly folded note sat on top—a departure announcement, sterile and final. My throat tightened, a strange, dry lump forming just behind my sternum. I managed a small, jerky nod, the movement feeling clumsy and inadequate. I wanted to reach out, to ask if he was alright, but the words caught, snagged in my chest like dry thread. I simply turned, the sound of my own footsteps echoing too loudly in the quiet space, and walked toward the door, my shoulders slumped as if the weight of the world had suddenly doubled.

[story 2]

I reread the email three times, the screen glare making the text swim. Retirement. It was just a word, yet it felt like a physical blow, a sudden drop in temperature. My hands began to tremble, the fine motor control I usually relied on dissolving into a faint, useless vibration. I stared at the framed photograph on his shelf—us, laughing on a ridiculous fishing trip—and felt a sharp, unexpected ache bloom in my chest. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to anchor myself, but the air seemed too thin. I picked up my pen, intending to start a new project, but my hand shook so violently I nearly dropped it. I just sat there, watching the dust motes dance in the afternoon light, a profound emptiness settling over me.

[story 3]

When I finally found him in the breakroom, he was nowhere to be seen. Just a hastily printed memo tacked to the corkboard, announcing his departure from the firm. I stood there, the paper trembling slightly in my grip, my gaze fixed on the spot where his usual coffee mug rested. A dull, heavy sensation pressed down on my shoulders, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I cleared my throat, the sound rasping in the quiet room. "Mr. Harrison?" My voice sounded thin, reedy. He didn't look up from his phone, and in that deliberate avoidance, I felt a profound sense of failure, as if I had somehow missed the signal, failed to recognize the significance of this ending. I slowly lowered the paper, the movement weighted with unspoken regret.
